items.q: Why is Magna Carta still cited in 'Power and the People' history?
items.correct: Because clauses on lawful judgement and protection from arbitrary imprisonment were reinterpreted in the 17th century to support broader liberties, even though they originally protected only barons.
items.misconception: Magna Carta directly created modern human rights in 1215.
items.random_wrong: Magna Carta gave the right to vote to all English men.
items.why: Its symbolic power grew over time, especially during the 17th-century constitutional struggles. Students confuse historical use with original meaning — a key historiographical insight.

items.q: Why was the Spanish Armada defeated in 1588?
items.correct: A combination of English tactics and fireships, poor Spanish planning, and the 'Protestant wind' storms that wrecked the fleet on the return.
items.misconception: Drake defeated the Armada single-handedly in a great sea battle.
items.random_wrong: Elizabeth I personally commanded the English fleet at sea.
items.why: Drake is mythologised. The English fleet harassed but did not destroy the Armada in battle — most Spanish ships were lost to storms around Scotland and Ireland.

items.q: What did the Wannsee Conference of January 1942 decide?
items.correct: It coordinated the bureaucracy of the already-decided Final Solution across ministries, scaling existing mass murder into a systematic Europe-wide extermination programme.
items.misconception: Wannsee was the meeting where the Nazis first decided to murder all Jews.
items.random_wrong: Wannsee was the meeting where Hitler personally launched WWII.
items.why: Mass shootings had begun in 1941; the decision predates Wannsee. The conference organised what was already happening — students treat it as the starting point when it was a coordination meeting.

items.correct: The voyage from Africa to the Americas
items.why: It's The voyage from Africa to the Americas. “A trade road in Europe” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which crop was most important on Caribbean plantations?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Sugar. “Wheat” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many legs (sides) did the triangle trade have?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
